h2: A city becomes strong before the crisis
p: Every mayor knows that a crisis rarely arrives on a neat schedule.
p_2: Sometimes the power goes out. Sometimes the internet goes down. Sometimes payments stop working. Sometimes a cyberattack hits a municipality, a hospital, a port company or an energy utility. Extreme weather, the threat of war, sabotage and disinformation can also disrupt a city.
p_html: Rotterdam is therefore preparing with a Resilience Programme. The municipality wants the city, its neighbourhoods and its own organisation to be better ready for major disruptions. [1]
p_3: That lesson matters not only for Rotterdam. It matters for the whole of Europe.
p_4: A city does not become resilient during a crisis. A city becomes resilient in the years, months and weeks before. In everyday conversations. In good agreements. In businesses that know each other. In neighbourhoods where people know who needs help. In municipalities that keep communicating, even when systems fail.
p_6: That is more than a nice line. It is a way of working.
p_7: Resilience can sound like a big word. But really it is about something very practical.
p_8: Can the municipality reach residents when the internet fails? Can a care provider keep running when suppliers arrive late? Can a supermarket stay open when card payments stop? Does a logistics company know whom to call when a port route closes? Does an elderly resident know where to find help?
p_9: These are not abstract questions. They decide whether a city stays calm when pressure builds.
p_html_2: In its programme Rotterdam names, among other things, geopolitical tensions, hybrid threats, digital attacks and the disruption of vital processes such as power, data, telephony and internet. [1]
p_10: Behind those words is ordinary life. A parent who cannot reach a child. A business owner who cannot deliver. A family doctor without access to systems. A neighbourhood team that does not know which residents need extra help.
p_11: That is why crisis preparation must be understandable. Not only for specialists, but for everyone.
p_12: A city is not only a town hall and a network of streets. A city is also a network of businesses.
p_13: Think of transport companies, port operators, installers, supermarkets, care suppliers, construction firms, energy companies, telecom providers, security firms, food producers and small entrepreneurs in the neighbourhood.
p_14: They matter not only for the economy. They matter for safety and continuity as well.
p_15: When a regional business fails, it can directly affect residents. When a cold chain breaks, it affects food. When an installer is unavailable, repairs take longer. When a logistics company halts, medicines, parts or groceries arrive late. When a local IT firm is unprepared, several clients can be hit at once.
p_16: Good cooperation therefore starts before the crisis.
p_17: A mayor can invite businesses to the table. But businesses can also take the first step themselves. They can map their dependencies. They can practise power or internet outages. They can make agreements with suppliers and customers. They can take part in regional crisis exercises.
p_18: Resilience is not only a public responsibility. It is also entrepreneurship.
p_19: ESRM stands for Enterprise Security Risk Management.
p_20: It sounds technical, but the idea is simple.
p_21: ESRM means: look together at what must be protected, which risks matter most and who takes responsibility.
p_22: It is not only about security. It is also about people, information, buildings, suppliers, digital systems, vital services and trust.
p_23: For a city, ESRM means that the municipality, businesses, emergency services and civic organisations do not look at risks separately. They try to understand together what happens when one element fails.
p_24: A cyberattack on a port company, a power outage in a neighbourhood and confusion caused by disinformation may look like three different problems. But in a real crisis they can reinforce each other.
p_25: ESRM helps to see those connections sooner.
p_26: In short: ESRM makes resilience manageable.
p_27: For businesses, ESRM is also practical. It helps them not only ask, "Are we protected?" but also, "Which customers, residents or supply chains are affected if we fail?"
p_28: That is a different way of thinking. And it is exactly the thinking we need in a time when risks are increasingly connected.
h2_5: The port makes this a European matter
p_html_3: Rotterdam is a city, but it is also a European hub. The port holds a strategic position in the Netherlands and Europe. [1]
p_html_4: Goods, energy, raw materials and logistics flows all pass through the port. In a serious international scenario, Rotterdam may also play a role in military transit and support for allies. [1]
p_29: That makes the city strong. But it also makes the city vulnerable.
p_30: When a port, a rail line, a bridge, a data link or an energy supply is hit, it is not felt by a single municipality alone. It is felt by businesses, regions and countries.
p_31: Crisis preparation is therefore not a local hobby. It is European security in practice.
p_32: Every port city can learn from this. So can every border region. Every industrial region. Every city with a hospital, a data centre, an energy hub or a major logistics function.
p_33: During a major disruption, help does not always arrive everywhere at the same time.
p_34: The first hours are often local. Sometimes even street by street.
p_html_5: Rotterdam is therefore working on neighbourhood resilience and emergency hubs where residents can turn during a crisis for information, help and basic services. [1]
p_36: A crisis is not only resolved in a crisis centre. A crisis is also absorbed by neighbours, neighbourhood teams, entrepreneurs, schools, faith communities, sports clubs and local businesses.
p_37: A baker with a generator can matter. A transport company with drivers can matter. A supermarket manager with local knowledge can matter. An installation firm that knows where the vulnerable buildings are can matter.
p_38: In many crisis plans such people are not central. But in reality they can make the difference.
p_39: Not everyone starts from the same position.
p_40: Some people have money for an emergency kit. Others do not. Some people understand official websites. Others do not. Some people have family nearby. Others are alone. Some residents do not speak the language of the authorities well.
p_html_6: Rotterdam therefore works together with organisations such as Voedselbank Rotterdam and Stichting Salaam to make emergency kits available to people with fewer financial resources. [1]
p_42: A city is only truly resilient when vulnerable people are included too.
p_43: The same applies to businesses. Large companies often have departments for risk, safety and continuity. Small businesses usually do not. Yet small entrepreneurs are essential in neighbourhoods and regional supply chains.
p_44: They must be able to take part as well.
p_45: That calls for simple language, practical agreements and short exercises. Not thick reports, but useful questions.
p_46: What must keep working? Whom do you need? Who needs you? What do you do if power, internet or staff fail?
p_47: When municipalities help businesses with this, the whole region grows stronger.
p_48: In a crisis, people look for something to hold on to.
p_49: They want to know what is happening. They want to know what is safe. They want to know where help is. They want to know whether they can travel, pay, call or use water.
p_html_7: Rotterdam is therefore looking at emergency communications, including through KPN's National Crisis Network and possible additions such as satellite communications and MESH networks. [1]
p_51: Communication must also be understandable. Short. Honest. Multilingual. Repeatable. And delivered by people who are trusted.
p_52: A crisis message must not sound like policy jargon.
p_53: Not: "There is a disruption of vital infrastructure."
p_54: But: "The power is out. Stay inside if you can. Check on your neighbours. For help, go to the emergency point at the school."
p_55: Businesses have a role here too. Employers reach staff. Shops reach customers. Care organisations reach clients. Port companies reach suppliers. Local entrepreneurs reach the neighbourhood.
p_56: In a crisis, communication is not only a government task. It is a shared responsibility.
p_57: A mayor does not have to wait for perfect plans.
p_58: The first step is to open the conversation. With residents. With businesses. With emergency services. With schools. With healthcare. With civic organisations. With the region.
p_59: Not with the question: "Who is responsible if things go wrong?"
p_60: But with the question: "What must we keep going together?"
p_61: That conversation changes the tone. It makes crisis preparation less legalistic and more human. Less distant and more practical.
p_62: Mayors can bring calm by making clear now what matters. They can invite businesses in as partners. They can take neighbourhoods seriously. They can demand plain language. They can treat exercises not as a box-ticking duty but as a way to build trust.
p_63: A city that practises together is less likely to panic.
p_64: Regional businesses do not have to wait for a crisis letter from the municipality.
p_66: Not with complex models, but with a simple conversation in the management team.
p_67: Which of our services must keep running? Which customers depend on us? Which suppliers do we need ourselves? What happens if the internet is out for two days? What happens if fuel becomes scarce? What happens if staff cannot get to work? Who decides then?
p_68: After that, the question becomes regional.
p_69: Which nearby businesses do we need? Which municipal contacts do we know? Which emergency services must know what we do? Can we mean something to the neighbourhood in a crisis?
p_70: For many businesses this is not a cost item. It is continuity. It is reputation. It is care for staff. It is responsibility for the region in which they work.
p_71: A prepared business does not only help itself. It helps the city.
p_72: Residents do not need to be afraid either.
p_73: Preparing is not panic. Preparing creates calm.
p_74: Know your neighbours. Think about water, light, medicines and important phone numbers. Know who on your street needs extra help. Make arrangements with your family. Follow trusted information from the municipality and emergency services.
p_75: But perhaps the most important thing is simpler still: don't wait for the crisis to meet the people around you.
p_76: A resilient neighbourhood starts with contact.
p_77: Rotterdam shows that crisis preparation consists of three layers of mutual coordination and cooperation.
p_78: The first is the strategic layer. It covers geopolitics, the port, vital infrastructure and hybrid threats.
p_79: The second is the organisational layer. It covers the municipality, crisis decision-making, emergency communications, continuity and cooperation with regional businesses.
p_80: The third is the human layer. It covers residents, entrepreneurs, neighbourhood networks, food banks, faith communities and trust.
p_81: These three layers must reinforce each other.
p_82: If the strategic layer is missing, a city sees major threats too late.
p_83: If the organisational layer is missing, responsibilities remain unclear.
p_84: If the human layer is missing, help does not reach the places where it is needed most.
p_86: Between government and business. Between port and city. Between the crisis organisation and neighbourhood networks. Between administrators and residents. Between plans on paper and people in practice.
p_87: That is why ESRM is useful. It helps administrators, businesses and civic partners to look together at risks, responsibilities and what must remain protected.
p_88: Europe talks a lot about security. But security also begins very close to home.
p_89: With a mayor who speaks plainly. With a port company that knows its role in the chain. With a neighbourhood that knows who needs help. With a regional business that keeps delivering when things get hard. With a municipality that can communicate even without the internet.
p_90: Rotterdam shows that resilience can be made practical.
p_91: Not in one grand plan that solves everything. But in many small steps that reinforce each other.
p_92: Street by street. Business by business. Neighbourhood by neighbourhood. City by city. Region by region.
p_93: The future will not become quieter on its own.
p_95: Not through fear. Not through panic. Not by writing thick reports alone.
p_96: But through preparation, cooperation and trust.
p_97: Rotterdam shows that a resilient city is not built by government alone. It is built by residents, businesses, civic organisations and administrators together.
p_98: The most important lesson is simple:
p_99: When things go wrong, no one should be on their own.
p_100: That is not only a message for Rotterdam.
title_tag: When things go wrong, you don't face it alone — ESRF.net
